courses.title: Why ChatGPT?
courses.description: Go from zero to actually useful with ChatGPT in 30 minutes. Hands-on from the first minute.

lessons.title: Meet ChatGPT
lessons.description: What ChatGPT is, how it thinks, what makes it powerful, and your first real interaction.

sections.content: Open chat.openai.com in another tab right now. Type: "What are you, and what can you actually do that's useful to me?" Notice how it reasons through your question instead of just searching for pages.

ChatGPT has three core strengths: versatile generation (write, code, analyze, translate across any domain), instruction-following (tracks every constraint you give it), and reasoning (works through trade-offs, not just pattern-matching). Use the "I want your honest assessment, including parts I might not want to hear" pattern to unlock more candid answers.

ChatGPT generates by reasoning, not searching. Three strengths: versatile generation, instruction-following, reasoning. To get honest answers, explicitly ask for critique. Next: how to talk to it effectively.

exercises.prompt: Pick the right ChatGPT mode for three different tasks.
exercises.correct_answer: {"title": "Pick the Right Mode", "steps": [{"type": "context", "text": "Three tasks. Each needs ChatGPT, but not the same ChatGPT feature. Match each to the right mode."}, {"type": "choice", "claude_response": "Task 1: Brainstorm 10 gift ideas for your partner's birthday. No files, just creative thinking.", "question": "Which mode?", "options": ["ChatGPT chat \u2014 browser conversation", "Canvas \u2014 collaborative document editor", "Code Interpreter \u2014 run code and analyze data"], "correct": 0, "feedback": "Pure thinking, no files, nothing to build. ChatGPT chat in the browser is exactly right."}, {"type": "choice", "claude_response": "Task 2: You have 6 months of sales data in an Excel file that needs analysis by region and month.", "question": "Which mode?", "options": ["ChatGPT chat \u2014 browser conversation", "Canvas \u2014 collaborative document editor", "Code Interpreter \u2014 upload the file and run Python analysis"], "correct": 2, "feedback": "Code Interpreter runs actual Python on your uploaded file and produces real analysis, charts, and summaries. This is exactly what it's for."}, {"type": "choice", "claude_response": "Task 3: You need to write a long technical proposal that you want to edit collaboratively with AI assistance.", "question": "Which mode?", "options": ["ChatGPT chat \u2014 browser conversation", "Canvas \u2014 persistent document with inline editing", "Code Interpreter \u2014 generate the document as code"], "correct": 1, "feedback": "Canvas gives you a persistent document workspace where you and ChatGPT edit together \u2014 ideal for long-form writing that needs revision and structure."}]}
exercises.explanation: The decision is one question: does this task need thinking, data analysis, or collaborative editing? Thinking = chat. Data/code = Code Interpreter. Long-form editing = Canvas.
hints: Ask yourself: is this a thinking task, a data task, or a long document editing task?

exercises.prompt: An AI gives you a confident answer about a legal question. You make a decision based on it. What's the real risk?
options: No risk -- AI is just a tool, the decision is yours
Confident wrong answers are dangerous because you're less likely to double-check them
The AI will learn from the mistake and improve
You can always reverse a bad decision
exercises.explanation: A confident wrong answer is worse than an uncertain one because you're less likely to question it. When AI flags uncertainty, you verify. When it states something as fact, you trust it. Prompting ChatGPT to flag its confidence level is a key skill.
hints: Think about the difference between an AI that says 'I think...' vs one that states something as absolute fact.

exercises.prompt: Write a prompt asking ChatGPT to review a job applicant's resume and give honest, useful feedback -- not just praise.
exercises.correct_answer: {"scenario":"You're hiring for a senior marketing role. A candidate's resume looks decent on the surface. Write a prompt that gets real feedback -- strengths AND weaknesses -- so you can decide whether to bring them in.","scoring_criteria":[{"keyword_group":["honest","critical","candid","don't hold back","genuine","real feedback","weaknesses","gaps","concerns","red flag"],"label":"Asked for honest/critical feedback","points":2},{"keyword_group":["senior","marketing","role","position","job","hiring"],"label":"Specified the role","points":1},{"keyword_group":["strengths","pros","strong","good","stands out","impressive"],"label":"Asked for strengths too (balanced review)","points":1},{"keyword_group":["interview","hire","decision","bring in","move forward","next step"],"label":"Connected to hiring decision","points":1},{"keyword_group":["experience","qualification","skill","background","achievement","metric","result"],"label":"Mentioned what to evaluate","points":1},{"keyword_group":["compare","versus","typical","expect","standard","benchmark","competitive"],"label":"Asked for benchmarking","points":1}],"max_points":7,"pass_threshold":4,"ideal_prompt":"I'm hiring for a senior marketing manager role. Here's a candidate's resume. Give me an honest assessment: what are the real strengths, what are the gaps or red flags, and would you recommend bringing them in for an interview? Don't be polite for the sake of it -- I need to make a good hiring decision.","tips":["Explicitly asking for weaknesses signals to ChatGPT that you want honesty, not flattery.","Connecting the review to a hiring decision gives ChatGPT a clear purpose for the feedback."]}
exercises.explanation: Most people ask AI to 'review my resume' and get 'Looks great!' When you explicitly ask for honest critique and tie it to a real decision, ChatGPT delivers the kind of feedback that actually helps.
hints: If you just say 'review this resume,' most AI will default to being nice. How do you signal you want the real assessment?

lessons.title: Your First Conversation
lessons.description: How to talk to ChatGPT so it actually helps. Context, iteration, and the prompting basics that matter.

sections.content: Open ChatGPT and type: "Write me an email." You'll get a generic template. Now try: "Write a follow-up email to Sarah who missed our Tuesday call. Warm but direct. Mention rescheduling for Thursday at 2pm. Under 100 words." Night and day. That's what context does.

Give ChatGPT five things upfront: Who (audience), What (deliverable), How (tone, format, length), Why (what decision this serves), What not (things to avoid). More context upfront = less editing after. Then iterate: tell it exactly what to change and what to keep.

Build this habit: ask ChatGPT to push back. "I'm thinking about X. What am I missing?" or "Poke holes in this plan before I commit." This pressure-tests your thinking before it matters — use it before big decisions and pitches.

Context turns generic output into useful output. Five elements: who, what, how, why, what not. Iterate with specific feedback. Ask it to push back before big decisions. Next: Custom Instructions and context windows.

exercises.prompt: What's wrong with the prompt 'Write me an email'?
options: Nothing -- ChatGPT can figure out what you mean
It's too short -- ChatGPT needs at least 100 words of instruction
It gives ChatGPT zero context about who it's to, what it's about, or what tone to use
You should never ask AI to write emails
exercises.explanation: Without context, ChatGPT has to guess everything: recipient, topic, tone, length, purpose. You'll get a generic template. Context is the difference between useful output and filler.
hints: Think about what you'd need to know if someone asked YOU to write an email with no other information.

exercises.prompt: Your client needs a project proposal by Friday. Write a detailed prompt to ChatGPT with enough context to get a strong first draft.
exercises.correct_answer: {"scenario":"Your client (a mid-size law firm) needs a proposal for a website redesign. Budget: $15K, timeline: 6 weeks, due Friday. The client is traditional and formal. Write a prompt that gives ChatGPT everything it needs to draft a proposal you could actually send.","scoring_criteria":[{"keyword_group":["law firm","client","legal","attorney","firm"],"label":"Identified the client type","points":1},{"keyword_group":["proposal","pitch","scope","deliverable"],"label":"Specified document type","points":1},{"keyword_group":["$15,000","15000","budget","cost","price","pricing"],"label":"Included budget","points":1},{"keyword_group":["6 weeks","six weeks","timeline","deadline","schedule","friday"],"label":"Mentioned timeline","points":1},{"keyword_group":["formal","professional","traditional","conservative","tone"],"label":"Specified tone or audience","points":2},{"keyword_group":["website","redesign","web","site","design"],"label":"Described the project","points":1}],"max_points":7,"pass_threshold":4,"ideal_prompt":"Write a project proposal for a website redesign for a mid-size law firm. Budget: $15K. Timeline: 6 weeks. The firm is traditional and formal -- no startup language. Include: executive summary, scope with deliverables, timeline with milestones, pricing breakdown. Keep it under 2 pages. Tone: professional and confident.","tips":["Tell ChatGPT who the audience is. A law firm and a tech startup need completely different tones.","Include constraints like page length, budget, and timeline -- they shape the entire document."]}
exercises.explanation: A detailed prompt with client type, budget, timeline, tone, and structure gives ChatGPT enough to produce a draft you'd actually review and send. Every decision you make upfront saves you editing time on the back end.
hints: Think about every decision ChatGPT would need to make without asking you. The more you answer upfront, the better the first draft.

exercises.prompt: You have 45 seconds. Write a prompt asking ChatGPT to push back on a bad business idea.
exercises.correct_answer: {"time_limit_seconds":45,"scenario":"Your friend wants to open a third coffee shop in a town of 5,000 people that already has two. They're asking you for money. Write a prompt that gets ChatGPT to challenge the idea, not encourage it.","scoring_criteria":[{"keyword_group":["push back","challenge","poke holes","devil's advocate","critique","problems","risks","why this won't work","honest"],"label":"Asked ChatGPT to challenge the idea","points":2},{"keyword_group":["coffee","shop","cafe","store","business"],"label":"Referenced the business type","points":1},{"keyword_group":["5000","small town","population","market","demand","two","competition","already","existing"],"label":"Mentioned market size or competition","points":2},{"keyword_group":["invest","money","friend","fund","capital","asking"],"label":"Referenced the investment ask","points":1},{"keyword_group":["question","ask","conversation","talk","discuss","bring up"],"label":"Connected to a real conversation","points":1}],"max_points":7,"pass_threshold":4,"ideal_prompt":"My friend wants to open a third coffee shop in a town of 5,000 people and is asking me to invest. Two coffee shops already exist. Be brutally honest: what are the biggest risks and reasons this could fail? Don't encourage the idea -- I need the hard questions before putting money in."}
exercises.explanation: Under time pressure, you find out what you naturally include. The key elements: tell ChatGPT to challenge (not encourage), give the relevant facts, and explain why you need honesty.
hints: Focus on telling ChatGPT what role to play: critic, not cheerleader.

lessons.title: Custom Instructions & Context
lessons.description: How ChatGPT's context window works, how Custom Instructions shape every response, and how to give ChatGPT the right information every time.

sections.content: Paste a long email thread into ChatGPT and ask: "What's the actual decision being made here, and who's blocking it?" It reads the entire thread and answers in 30 seconds. That's the context window — how much ChatGPT holds in active memory. Modern ChatGPT can hold entire contracts or research papers in one conversation.

Custom Instructions let you brief ChatGPT once so you don't repeat yourself every session. Click your profile → Custom Instructions. Be specific over general. Include examples of good output. State what NOT to do — negative constraints are often the most powerful. Template: "About me: I am [role]. My audience is [audience]. Always [constraint]. Never [exclusion]."

The context window holds entire documents in memory. Custom Instructions give ChatGPT persistent defaults. Be specific, include examples, state exclusions. Next: what ChatGPT is genuinely great at and where it struggles.

exercises.prompt: You're reviewing a 40-page contract. Write a prompt that uses ChatGPT's context window to cross-reference two distant sections.
exercises.correct_answer: {"scenario":"You've pasted a 40-page vendor contract into ChatGPT. The indemnification clause on page 30 seems to create unlimited liability, but you remember the liability cap on page 5. Write a prompt that asks ChatGPT to cross-reference these two sections.","scoring_criteria":[{"keyword_group":["page 5","earlier","section","liability","cap","limit","previous","above","before"],"label":"Referenced an earlier part of the document","points":2},{"keyword_group":["indemnification","indemnify","page 30","current","this section","this clause"],"label":"Referenced the current section","points":1},{"keyword_group":["conflict","contradict","override","weaken","undermine","loophole","exception","cross-reference","compare","connect"],"label":"Asked about conflicts or connections","points":2},{"keyword_group":["negotiate","change","fix","recommend","should I","what should"],"label":"Asked for actionable recommendation","points":1},{"keyword_group":["protect","risk","exposure","liable","dangerous","concern","gap"],"label":"Focused on practical impact","points":1}],"max_points":7,"pass_threshold":4,"ideal_prompt":"The indemnification clause on page 30 seems to create unlimited liability in some scenarios. But the liability cap on page 5 was supposed to limit our exposure. Cross-reference these two sections: does the indemnification clause effectively override the liability cap? If so, what language creates the loophole and what should I push back on?","tips":["Referencing specific pages forces ChatGPT to use its full context window.","Asking about conflicts between sections is the context window's killer feature."]}
exercises.explanation: Cross-referencing distant parts of a document is the context window's killer feature. ChatGPT holds the entire document at once and can compare sections systematically.
hints: The power move is connecting two distant parts of the document.

exercises.prompt: Walk through setting up Custom Instructions that make ChatGPT consistently useful for your specific role.
exercises.correct_answer: {"title": "Setting Up Custom Instructions", "steps": [{"type": "context", "text": "You're a product manager who uses ChatGPT daily for writing, analysis, and decision-making. Let's set up Custom Instructions that make every conversation start in the right context."}, {"type": "choice", "claude_response": "You open Custom Instructions for the first time. What's the most important thing to include in 'What should ChatGPT know about you'?", "question": "What belongs in the first field?", "options": ["Your name and location", "Your role, industry, and the type of work you do with ChatGPT", "Your favorite programming language", "Nothing — leave it blank"], "correct": 1, "feedback": "Your role and context is the foundation. 'I'm a product manager at a B2B SaaS company. I write specs, analyze user research, prep for stakeholder meetings, and draft strategic documents' — this shapes every response."}, {"type": "choice", "claude_response": "Now you need to fill in 'How would you like ChatGPT to respond?' What's the most valuable thing to specify here?", "question": "What response preference makes the most difference?", "options": ["Always respond in bullet points", "Your tone preferences, length defaults, and what to avoid (anti-patterns)", "Always start with a summary", "Use formal language always"], "correct": 1, "feedback": "Tone + length defaults + anti-patterns. 'Be direct. Lead with the answer, then explain. Avoid corporate jargon like leverage or synergy. Default to under 200 words unless I ask for more.' These three things filter out 80% of bad outputs."}]}
exercises.explanation: Custom Instructions work best when they combine your role context, tone preferences, length defaults, and explicit anti-patterns. Set once, applies to every conversation.
hints: Think about what you'd want ChatGPT to assume about you without being asked every time.

exercises.prompt: What happens when an AI runs out of context window space during a long conversation?
options: It tells you it forgot and asks you to repeat
It silently drops earlier context and starts giving contradictory answers
It automatically saves and reloads the conversation
Nothing -- modern AI never runs out of context
exercises.explanation: When context is exceeded, AI silently drops earlier parts of the conversation. It doesn't warn you. It just starts giving answers that contradict what it said before, or misses connections to earlier discussion. That's why context window size matters for real work.
hints: Think about what 'forgetting' means for an AI. Does it know it forgot?

lessons.title: ChatGPT's Strengths & Limits
lessons.description: What ChatGPT is genuinely great at, where it struggles, and how to work around the gaps.

sections.content: ChatGPT excels at versatile generation (write, code, analyze, translate fluently across domains), multi-constraint instruction-following (tracks tone, format, length, inclusions, exclusions simultaneously), and reasoning through trade-offs. Its integrated toolkit extends this: DALL-E for images, Browse for live web search, Code Interpreter for real Python on your files, Canvas for long-form editing.

Where ChatGPT struggles: hallucination on obscure facts (verify names, dates, citations through primary sources), stale information without Browse enabled (paste current data in yourself), and large-scale computation (use Code Interpreter or a spreadsheet for the math, then paste results into chat for interpretation).

Strengths: generation, instruction-following, reasoning, integrated tools. Limits: verify obscure facts, enable Browse for current data, use Code Interpreter for computation. The skill is routing each task to the right tool. Next: turning casual use into real workflows.

exercises.prompt: Which ChatGPT feature would you use to analyze an uploaded sales CSV, calculate revenue by region, and create a bar chart?
options: Chat -- describe the analysis in words
Code Interpreter -- it runs actual Python on your file and produces real charts
Browse with Bing -- search for sales analysis tools
DALL-E -- generate an image of a bar chart
exercises.explanation: Code Interpreter runs actual Python on uploaded files. You get real computed results and downloadable charts — not just descriptions of how to do the analysis. This is the tool for actual data work.
hints: Which feature runs actual code on your actual file?

exercises.prompt: You have a 50-page contract to review before signing. Write a prompt that gets ChatGPT to find the 3 biggest risks.
exercises.correct_answer: {"scenario":"You're about to sign a 50-page SaaS vendor contract. You want ChatGPT to read the entire thing and identify the 3 biggest risks -- clauses that could hurt you most.","scoring_criteria":[{"keyword_group":["risk","dangerous","concern","problem","issue","flag","watch out","trap","hidden"],"label":"Asked for risks specifically","points":2},{"keyword_group":["3","three","top","biggest","most important","worst"],"label":"Specified number of risks","points":1},{"keyword_group":["contract","agreement","vendor","SaaS","document"],"label":"Referenced the document type","points":1},{"keyword_group":["liability","termination","auto-renewal","indemnification","penalty","clause","section"],"label":"Mentioned specific areas to check","points":1},{"keyword_group":["why","explain","impact","consequence","what happens","cost","exposure"],"label":"Asked for explanation of impact","points":1},{"keyword_group":["negotiate","push back","change","fix","recommend","suggest","alternative"],"label":"Asked for negotiation recommendations","points":1}],"max_points":7,"pass_threshold":4,"ideal_prompt":"Read this 50-page SaaS vendor contract and identify the 3 biggest risks to my company. For each: (1) quote the clause, (2) explain why it's dangerous in plain English, (3) suggest what I should negotiate instead. Focus on liability caps, auto-renewal traps, termination restrictions, and indemnification.","tips":["Tell ChatGPT what areas to focus on so it knows where to dig deepest.","Requesting negotiation alternatives turns analysis into action."]}
exercises.explanation: A good contract review prompt tells ChatGPT what to look for (risks, not summaries), how many, and what to do with them. That turns a 50-page document into 3 actionable items.
hints: Don't just ask ChatGPT to 'review' the contract. Tell it what you're worried about.

exercises.prompt: Match each task to the best ChatGPT tool or feature for the job.
exercises.correct_answer: {"Analyze an uploaded CSV and create a revenue chart":"Code Interpreter","Generate a product mockup image from a description":"DALL-E","Get today's news on a merger that was announced this morning":"Browse with Bing","Review a contract for hidden risks":"Chat (paste the contract)","Write a long proposal with collaborative editing":"Canvas","Use a pre-built AI tuned for legal document review":"Custom GPT"}
exercises.explanation: Each ChatGPT tool has a specific use case: Code Interpreter for data/code, DALL-E for images, Browse for live info, Canvas for long-form editing, Custom GPTs for specialized tasks. The best tool depends on the task.
hints: Think about what each task actually requires: data analysis, visual creation, live information, or long-form editing?

lessons.title: From Chatting to Building
lessons.description: How casual ChatGPT conversations become real workflows. The bridge to Tracks 2-4.

sections.content: Power users use ChatGPT as part of repeatable processes, not one-off questions. Two patterns to start now: Research-to-decision — paste five articles, ask where they agree and conflict and what they suggest for your situation. Draft-review-ship — get a draft, then ask ChatGPT to critique it from your reader's perspective before you polish and send.

After a productive session, ask: "Write me a reusable template I could use next time I face a similar situation." Save it. Next time, paste the template into a new conversation with fresh specifics. This is how power users build a personal playbook — not by memorizing techniques, but by capturing what worked.

Track 1 complete. You know how ChatGPT works, how to give it context, what it's good at, and how casual use becomes repeatable workflows. Three milestones for your first month: save your best prompt, set up Custom Instructions, build one Custom GPT for a recurring task. Go build something.

exercises.prompt: Walk through the draft-review-ship loop: start with a vague prompt, iterate through three rounds until the output is great.
exercises.explanation: Two rounds of iteration took a generic post to something with a real voice and honest self-critique. The lesson: your first prompt is never your last. The magic is in the back-and-forth.
hints: Each round should change something specific. Don't just say 'better' -- say what to change and what to keep.

exercises.prompt: You have 45 seconds. Write a prompt for getting ChatGPT to honestly review your business plan before an investor pitch.
exercises.correct_answer: {"time_limit_seconds":45,"scenario":"You have a 10-page business plan for a new SaaS product. You're pitching investors next week. Write a prompt that gets ChatGPT to tear it apart -- find the weak spots before the investors do.","scoring_criteria":[{"keyword_group":["honest","brutal","critical","tough","don't hold back","tear apart","weaknesses","holes","problems"],"label":"Asked for honest/critical review","points":2},{"keyword_group":["investor","pitch","funding","VC","raise","present"],"label":"Mentioned the investor context","points":1},{"keyword_group":["business plan","plan","SaaS","product","startup"],"label":"Referenced the document","points":1},{"keyword_group":["question","ask","challenge","object","pushback","concern"],"label":"Asked for likely investor questions","points":2},{"keyword_group":["fix","improve","strengthen","recommend","suggest","change"],"label":"Asked for improvement suggestions","points":1}],"max_points":7,"pass_threshold":4,"ideal_prompt":"I'm pitching investors next week on this 10-page SaaS business plan. Be my toughest critic: find every weak assumption, questionable projection, and gap an investor will attack. For each weakness, tell me what the investor will say and how I should fix the plan or prepare to answer. Don't be encouraging -- I need the hard truth now, not in the meeting."}
exercises.explanation: The best prompts for honest review explicitly ask ChatGPT NOT to be nice. Tell it the stakes, the role, and what to do with the findings.
hints: Think: what would you want a brutally honest friend to tell you before a big pitch?

exercises.prompt: Which workflow pattern turns ChatGPT from a one-off tool into an operating system?
options: Asking longer questions each time
Using ChatGPT only for creative brainstorming
Building a Custom GPT with persistent context and running the same analysis weekly with fresh data
Switching between ChatGPT and other AI tools for different tasks
exercises.explanation: The Custom GPT operator pattern -- persistent context, recurring analysis, fresh data -- turns ChatGPT from a chatbot into a decision-support system. The Custom GPT knows your role, your audience, and your format. You just add the latest inputs.
hints: Which option describes a repeatable process, not a one-off conversation?
